docket_entries.title: Batch of 29 Public Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: Twenty-nine Minnesota residents submitted identical form letter comments urging rejection of the BlackRock acquisition. The standard text argues: 'Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.' They cite two examples of alleged private equity impacts: Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin (claiming 'double-digit rate increases every two years') and Upper Peninsula Power Company in Michigan (claiming 'similarly large rate increases'). The comments assert that 'Approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.' These are ideological objections to private equity ownership generally, not conditional opposition offering specific remedies. One commenter (Eric Johnson) added: 'This is nothing more than monopoly control.'
key_arguments: Private equity ownership of utilities inherently prioritizes profits over public service ('Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers')
Historical precedent shows private equity utility ownership leads to double-digit rate increases (citing Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin and Upper Peninsula Power Company in Michigan)
Approval would set precedent encouraging private equity acquisition of utilities nationwide ('open the floodgates')
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Introduces public opposition theme: 'Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.' Cites Superior Water, Light as example of private equity impact. First significant batch of identical public comments opposing acquisition on principle of private equity utility ownership.
key_excerpts: Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.

When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.

docket_entries.title: Batch Filing: 30 Retail Customers Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: 30 individual Minnesota residents submitted identical form letters urging the Commission to 'reject the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power.' The template argues: 'Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.' All cite identical examples of alleged private equity rate increases at Superior Water, Light and Power (Wisconsin) and Upper Peninsula Power Company (Michigan), claiming 'double-digit rate increases every two years.' The letters warn that 'Approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.' No filer provides individual circumstances, specific proposed conditions, or unique arguments. This is a form letter campaign with broad ideological opposition to private equity utility ownership, not substantive regulatory intervention.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership of utilities inherently leads to rate increases, citing Superior Water, Light & Power (Wisconsin) and Upper Peninsula Power Company (Michigan) examples with alleged 'double-digit rate increases every two years'
Approval would create precedent enabling 'floodgates' for private equity acquisition of utilities nationwide
Utilities are 'essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers' and should not be owned by private equity firms
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates Entry #42 arguments with identical language: 'reject the BlackRock takeover' and 'Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.' Same private equity rate increase examples cited. No new substantive arguments beyond additional signature count.
key_excerpts: Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.

When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.
Approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 30 Public Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: 30 Minnesota residents submitted public comments opposing the transaction, nearly all using identical or near-identical templated language. The core argument is that 'utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers' and that private equity ownership leads to rate increases, citing examples from Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin (double-digit rate increases every two years) and Upper Peninsula Power Company in Michigan. Commenters express concern that 'approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.' The opposition is ideological, based on general opposition to private equity ownership of utilities rather than specific conditions that would make this transaction acceptable.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership of utilities inevitably leads to rate increases, citing Superior Water, Light and Power (Wisconsin) with 'double-digit rate increases every two years' and Upper Peninsula Power Company (Michigan) with similar increases
Utilities are essential public services that should not be owned by Wall Street moneymakers, with approval creating a dangerous precedent for private equity targeting utilities nationwide
Approval would harm ratepayers, particularly those on fixed incomes, who are already 'strained at the seams'
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Uses same template as Entries #42-43: 'utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers' and identical private equity concerns. Represents growing volume of opposition but no new arguments or evidence beyond prior batches.
key_excerpts: When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.
Approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 30 Individual Customer Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: Batch of 30 individual customer comments opposing the BlackRock acquisition. Common themes include: (1) Opposition to private equity ownership of utilities as a matter of principle ('Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers'), (2) Concerns about rate increases based on examples from Wisconsin and Michigan ('When private equity took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years'), (3) Loss of local accountability and transparency ('Control of this vital utility should remain accountable to the people of our state—not to a global investment firm whose primary obligation is to its shareholders'), (4) Fears that BlackRock's profit focus will compromise service quality and clean energy commitments, and (5) One commenter (Chloe Olson) notes that 'BlackRock would acquire 10 out of 13 board member seats' giving them control over key decisions. Several cite private equity playbook of making promises to close deals then abandoning them. Most are form letter style comments. No specific conditions requested—commenters want outright rejection.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership of utilities leads to rate increases: 'When private equity took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases'

Utilities should not be privatized: 'Utilities are a monopoly by geography and as such should never be privatized. Giving up control of electricity or water infrastructure to a private company will always come back to haunt you'

Private equity firms prioritize short-term shareholder returns over long-term infrastructure and clean energy: 'Private equity or investment firms often focus on short-term returns, which can come at the expense of necessary long-term infrastructure investments and ratepayer protections'
Loss of local accountability: 'Control of this vital utility should remain accountable to the people of our state—not to a global investment firm whose primary obligation is to its shareholders'

Private equity acquisition playbook involves false promises: 'The purchasing company will say anything and agree to anything to close the deal. But once that is done those promises will go away and the rules will be lobbied away'
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Continues same two themes from Entries #42-44: '(1) Opposition to private equity ownership of utilities as a matter of principle' and '(2) Concerns about rate increases based on examples.' No substantive variation from prior template submissions.
key_excerpts: When private equity took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.
Utilities are a monopoly by geography and as such should never be privatized. Giving up control of electricity or water infrastructure to a private company will always come back to haunt you.

With this acquisition, BlackRock would acquire 10 out of 13 board member seats, thereby gaining significantly more control over key decisions that would affect one of Minnesota's essential utilities. This raises serious concerns about rate increases and the prioritization of profit over public interest.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 13 Retail Customer Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: 13 retail customers oppose the BlackRock acquisition based on concerns about private equity ownership of public utilities. Common themes include: (1) Fear of rate increases - 'Minnesotans can't afford to pay their shareholders and the actual cost of power. Energy costs will skyrocket'; (2) Distrust of BlackRock's promises - 'they don't keep promises. They have more money than the state of MN so a legal battle wouldn't go well if they had power'; (3) Preference for public ownership - 'If anything we need the state of MN to acquire it and run it for the people'; (4) Concerns about AI data centers - Ella Springer worries about 'the unregulated use of power, and the construction of AI plants along Lake Superior'; (5) Ideological opposition to private equity in utilities - 'Private equity has NO business owning a public utility' and 'Public utilities should NEVER be owned in any way by for profit groups.' Multiple commenters characterize BlackRock as an 'evil company that monopolizes then jacks up prices' and express concern about loss of local Minnesota control.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership will lead to rate increases because customers will pay both shareholder returns and actual power costs: 'Minnesotans can't afford to pay their shareholders and the actual cost of power. Energy costs will skyrocket'

BlackRock's financial resources exceed Minnesota's ability to enforce commitments: 'They have more money than the state of MN so a legal battle wouldn't go well if they had power such as ownership and don't hold up their end of the agreement'
AI data center development threatens Lake Superior and increases unregulated power consumption
Public utilities should remain in public or local control, not be owned by for-profit corporations
BlackRock will prioritize profits over infrastructure maintenance and public interest
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates opposition with familiar themes: 'Minnesotans can't afford to pay their shareholders and the actual cost of power. Energy costs will skyrocket' and distrust of private equity. Same conceptual arguments as Entries #42-45 with slight wording variations.
key_excerpts: They will cause irreparable damage to utilities if they get into them. Please don't let them get their hands on a monopolized resource. Minnesotans can't afford to pay their shareholders and the actual cost of power. Energy costs will skyrocket, they don't keep promises.

I worry about the risk of increased prices on power, the unregulated use of power, and the construction of AI plants along Lake Superior. I ask the board to consider the best interests of Minnesotans, not temporary profits.
They have more money than the state of MN so a legal battle wouldn't go well if they had power such as ownership and don't hold up their end of the agreement.

docket_entries.title: Updated Stipulation: Major Settlement with $100M+ in Commitments and Broad Stakeholder Support
docket_entries.entry_summary: Minnesota Power files an expanded settlement package incorporating most Commissioner decision options proposed on September 16, 2025, achieving support from 11 parties and stakeholders. The filing states: 'Through the commitments in the Settlement and the additional commitments outlined in Attachment A, the Partners have agreed to provide more than $100 million in rate credits and investor-funded investments for customers, in addition to a one-year rate case stay-out.' Key additions include: (1) $50 million in customer bill credits to be distributed by 2032, (2) $50 million Clean Firm Fund for clean energy resources to minimize gas additions and utilize Boswell interconnection, (3) $10 million Weatherization Fund for low-income customers prioritizing those using delivered fuel, (4) ROE cap at 9.78% and equity ratio cap at 53.0% through 2030, with authority to reduce ROE by up to 100 basis points for violations, (5) SEC-equivalent financial reporting through 2030 with independent audit oversight by Department of Commerce, (6) Board composition with 8 independent directors including 3 from Minnesota, and (7) Clean Firm Plan filing within 90 days to achieve carbon-free standard cost-effectively while minimizing stranded gas asset risk. Settling parties include Minnesota Power, DOC, GIP, CPP Investments, Energy CENTS Coalition, IBEW Local 31, IUOE Local 49, LiUNA, Fresh Energy, Clean Grid Alliance, Center for Energy and Environment, and Clean Energy Economy Minnesota. Critically, the filing notes that OAG-RUD, CUB, CURE, Sierra Club, and Large Power Intervenors all stated 'they would not support the Acquisition, and that the Commission's new Decision Options would not affect their opposition.' This coalition represents sufficient support for approval despite continued opposition from consumer advocates and environmental groups.
key_arguments: The expanded package provides over $100 million in direct customer benefits beyond the original settlement, demonstrating responsiveness to regulatory concerns and extraordinary value

The $50 million Clean Firm Fund enables Minnesota Power to meet the Carbon-Free Standard in the most cost-effective manner by funding clean firm capacity and minimizing natural gas additions that risk becoming stranded assets

ROE cap at 9.78% and equity cap at 53.0% through 2030, combined with penalty provisions allowing up to 100 basis point ROE reductions for violations, provide strong ratepayer protection against excessive returns under private ownership
Enhanced financial transparency through SEC-equivalent reporting and independent audit oversight by DOC addresses concerns about private company accountability through fiscal year 2030

The $10 million Weatherization Fund targets low-income customers using delivered fuel (propane, heating oil), addressing energy burden for the most vulnerable customers outside traditional ECO programs
Board composition with 8 independent directors and 3 Minnesota residents ensures local representation and governance independence

Broad stakeholder coalition including labor unions (IBEW, IUOE, LiUNA), low-income advocates (Energy CENTS), and clean energy groups (Fresh Energy, Clean Grid Alliance, CEE, CEEM) demonstrates public interest benefits across constituencies
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Expands settlement to incorporate 'most Commissioner decision options proposed on September 16, 2025' with '$100M+ in Commitments' achieving 'support from 11 parties and stakeholders.' This represents a major settlement expansion beyond prior stipulations, directly responding to regulatory concerns with specific financial commitments.
key_excerpts: Through the commitments in the Settlement and the additional commitments outlined in Attachment A, the Partners have agreed to provide more than $100 million in rate credits and investor-funded investments for customers, in addition to a one-year rate case stay-out.

Minnesota Power, along with GIP and CPP Investments, also reached out to each of the Office of the Attorney General-Residential Utilities Division, Citizens Utility Board (MN), CURE, Sierra Club, and Large Power Intervenors to determine whether they would support approval of the Acquisition or otherwise modify their positions should the Commission adopt any combination of the new Commissioner Decision Options. These parties each stated that they would not support the Acquisition, and that the Commission's new Decision Options would not affect their opposition to the Acquisition.

The Clean Firm Plan shall: Contemplate replacement and/or surplus use of the Boswell interconnection to achieve a lower emission scenario using the updated externality values. Minimize the size and capacity factor of natural gas resources added to minimize the risk of stranded assets for Minnesota Power customers. Include clean firm capacity to be funded by the $50 million Clean Firm Fund commitment.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 30 Retail Customer Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: 30 Minnesota Power customers filed comments urging rejection of the BlackRock acquisition. The comments focus on three core concerns: (1) Rate increases based on precedent - multiple commenters cite that 'when private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years' and similar increases occurred at Upper Peninsula Power Company in Michigan; (2) Private equity's profit motive is incompatible with essential public services - commenters argue 'utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers' and that BlackRock 'only cares about shareholder value'; (3) Concerns about a proposed Hermantown data center and its energy demands, with one commenter noting it would 'consume the equivalent amount of energy as over 30,000 homes' while data centers 'cut deals with power companies for cheap electricity by passing the financial burden off to household customers.' Several commenters reference BlackRock's own annual report discussing plans to create energy deficits through AI/data center expansion. The comments request outright denial with no conditions proposed.
key_arguments: Private equity takeovers of utilities have led to 'double-digit rate increases every two years' at Superior Water, Light and Power (Wisconsin) and Upper Peninsula Power Company (Michigan), establishing a pattern that will repeat in Minnesota

BlackRock's business model is fundamentally incompatible with utility service: 'The interests of private equity firms are often, if not always, in direct opposition to the interests of the public' and approval would consolidate 'financial wealth and political power into the hands of the few'

The timing with the proposed Hermantown data center (consuming energy equivalent to 30,000+ homes) suggests BlackRock will artificially increase demand through data centers that 'cut deals with power companies for cheap electricity by passing the financial burden off to household customers'

BlackRock's own annual report reveals their strategy of 'taking deliberate action to bring meaningful private market opportunities to our clients by leveraging growing public deficits' through expanding data centers to create energy shortfalls and investment opportunities
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Introduces concrete precedent argument: 'when private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw' rate increases. This is the first filing citing specific utility acquisition precedent with customer rate impact data, providing empirical basis for rate increase concerns beyond speculation.
key_excerpts: When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.

We can see from page 4 and pages 23-25 of BlackRock's annual statement that they are, in their own words, 'taking deliberate action…to bring meaningful private market opportunities to our clients' by leveraging 'growing public deficits'...This will be done by expanding data centers to new locations. The new locations are intended to outpace current energy infrastructure, creating an energy deficit and a future investing opportunity.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 29 Retail Customer Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: 29 Minnesota Power customers filed comments urging rejection of the BlackRock acquisition. Most comments follow a standard template arguing 'utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers' and citing rate increase precedents: 'When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.' Several commenters express concern that 'approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.' One commenter (Tara Stout, environmental scientist) provides more detailed opposition, arguing BlackRock's legal mandate to maximize shareholder profit is 'mutually exclusive' with utility public service obligations, citing multiple instances of alleged BlackRock greenwashing including Mississippi cease-and-desist (March 2024), Tennessee AG lawsuit settlement (January 2025), and collapse of environmental voting from 47% (2021) to 4% (2024). Several commenters emphasize lack of customer choice with utility monopolies.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership of utilities leads to double-digit rate increases every two years, as evidenced by Superior Water, Light and Power (Wisconsin) and Upper Peninsula Power Company (Michigan)
Utilities are natural monopolies where customers cannot choose another provider, therefore should remain public services rather than profit-maximization vehicles

BlackRock has documented history of ESG greenwashing including Mississippi regulatory cease-and-desist (March 2024), Tennessee AG lawsuit (settled January 2025), and collapse of environmental shareholder proposal support from 47% to 4%
Approving this deal would set precedent encouraging more private equity acquisitions of utilities nationwide
BlackRock's fiduciary duty to maximize shareholder returns is fundamentally incompatible with utility obligation to provide affordable service
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: 29 retail customers use template arguing 'utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.' Cite precedent: 'When private equity firms took over Superior Water' (specific rate increase example provided). Reinforces opposition pattern from prior comment batches.
key_excerpts: Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers. When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years.

BlackRock's legal mandate, by contrast, is to maximize profit for its shareholders. These goals are mutually exclusive. They are incompatible and cannot be reconciled. For this reason alone, this acquisition must be rejected.

History shows us that when monopoly power is left unchecked, private interests exploit public needs. This is why safeguards exist, to protect the public from exploitation and to guard against greenwashing and broken promises.

key_excerpts: Utilities are essential public services, not Wall Street moneymakers.

When private equity firms took over Superior Water, Light and Power in Wisconsin, customers saw double-digit rate increases every two years. In the Upper Peninsula of Michigan, a private equity firm bought the Upper Peninsula Power Company, and customers have seen similarly large rate increases.
Approving the BlackRock takeover of Minnesota Power would open the floodgates for more private equity firms to target utilities nationwide.

docket_entries.title: Commissioner Tuma Decision Options - ROE Cap, Compliance Filings, Arrearage Forgiveness
docket_entries.entry_summary: Commissioner Tuma proposes extending the ROE cap at 9.78% and equity ratio cap at 53.0% through December 31, 2030 (revising Commissioner Ham's earlier proposal). Critically, Tuma adds enforcement teeth: 'The Commission may reduce the rate of return on equity by up to 100 basis points if it finds any significant violation of conditions imposed in this docket.' This creates a penalty mechanism beyond standard enforcement. Tuma also proposes four mandatory compliance filings within 30 days of the order to operationalize vague settlement commitments: (1) defining the baseline for 'historical levels of economic development' that must be maintained, (2) establishing the base affordability budget amount that cannot be reduced, (3) providing specific details and tariff language for the arrearage forgiveness program reducing residential arrears to pre-COVID-19 levels, and (4) submitting a detailed work plan with milestones for developing an additional arrearage forgiveness program in collaboration with Consumer Affairs and Energy CENTS Coalition. These compliance filings are critical because they convert general settlement language into measurable, enforceable commitments with delegated approval authority to the Executive Secretary if no objections are filed within 14 days.
key_arguments: ROE and equity ratio caps should be extended through December 31, 2030 to provide longer-term consumer protection than other proposed options
Commission needs explicit enforcement authority to reduce ROE by up to 100 basis points for significant condition violations, strengthening compliance incentives beyond standard remedies

Settlement commitments regarding economic development, affordability budgets, and arrearage forgiveness contain undefined terms ('historical levels', 'base amount', 'similar offering') that must be quantified through compliance filings to be enforceable
Baseline metrics must be established within 30 days while facts are fresh and before the Company can claim changed circumstances make historical comparisons difficult
Arrearage forgiveness program addressing COVID-19 debt requires specific tariff language and work plan with stakeholder collaboration to ensure meaningful implementation
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Extends ROE cap to 9.78% and equity ratio cap at 53.0% through December 31, 2030 (longer timeline than Ham). Adds enforcement mechanism: 'The Commission may reduce the rate of return on equity by up to 100 basis points if it finds any' [violations]. First proposal with penalty provisions.
key_excerpts: Minnesota Power's rate of return on equity is capped at 9.78 percent (as approved in Docket No. E-015/GR-23-155) and Minnesota Power's equity ratio in its capital structure will be capped at 53.0 percent until December 31st, 2030.

The Commission may reduce the rate of return on equity by up to 100 basis points if it finds any significant violation of conditions imposed in this docket in addition to all other enforcement authorities granted in the Settlement Stipulation and Addendum and within the statutory authority of the Commission.

Within 30 days after the order, the Company shall make a compliance filing identifying the 'historical levels of economic development in the State of Minnesota' contemplated in the July 11, 2025 Settlement Stipulation at paragraph 1.66.

docket_entries.title: Commissioner Partridge Revised Decision Options - Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Fund
docket_entries.entry_summary: Commissioner Partridge proposes revised approval conditions 30 and 31 establishing a $10 million Long-term Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Weatherization Fund. The revision shows Partridge partially accepting Resolving Parties' September 24 proposals (indicated by blue text) while making additional modifications (red text). Key changes include: (1) rejecting water heating savings eligibility and efficient fuel switching language proposed by Resolving Parties; (2) accepting addition of 'load management enabling technology' as eligible measure; (3) shifting triennial plan approval authority from Commission to Department of Commerce. The fund targets income-qualified customers (defined by Minn. Stat. § 216B.2402) with priority for delivered fuel heating users. Alloy Parent must contribute $2 million annually starting January 2026, completing $10 million by January 2030. Eligible measures: pre-weatherization, building envelope efficiency, heat pump water heaters, air source heat pumps, and load management technology. Critical limitation: no ratepayer cost recovery for contributions or projects, and energy savings cannot count toward Minnesota Power's ECO financial incentives. Administrative costs capped at 5%. Department of Commerce gets approval authority over triennial implementation plans (first due June 1, 2026), with annual April 1 reporting on spending, participation, measures, and savings.
key_arguments: Fund should reduce space heating and cooling loads for eligible customers, separate from and additional to Minnesota Power's existing Energy Conservation and Optimization portfolio and state/federal weatherization programs
Neither fund contributions nor funded projects should be eligible for ratepayer cost recovery, ensuring merger-related benefits flow to customers not shareholders
Energy savings from the fund should not count toward Minnesota Power's ECO utility financial incentive, preventing double-counting of benefits
Among income-qualified customers, Minnesota Power should prioritize those using delivered fuel for heating, targeting highest energy burden households
Department of Commerce rather than Commission should approve triennial implementation plans, streamlining regulatory oversight while maintaining accountability through annual reporting
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Proposes conditions 30 and 31 establishing '$10 million Long-term Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Weatherization Fund.' Revision shows Partridge incorporating Resolving Parties' proposals (blue text notation) while adding modifications. First specific weatherization fund proposal with dollar amount.
key_excerpts: The objective of the Long-term, Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Fund is to reduce space heating and cooling loads for eligible Minnesota Power electric customers. The Long-term, Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Fund is separate from, and additional to, Minnesota Power's Energy Conservation and Optimization portfolio, state weatherization programs, and the federal Weatherization Assistance Program.

Neither contributions to the Long-term, Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Weatherization Fund nor portions of an investment or project financed with the Fund are eligible for cost recovery from Minnesota ratepayers.

The Weatherization Long-term, Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Fund will only be used to support investments in weatherization, and energy conservation, and efficient fuel switching electrification for Minnesota Power electric customers who qualify for low-income Energy Conservation and Optimization services, as determined by Minnesota Statutes § 216B.2402. Among eligible customers, Minnesota Power shall prioritize those who use delivered fuel for heating.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 40 Public Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition of Minnesota Power (ALLETE)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Batch filing of 40 public comments received outside the comment period opposing the BlackRock/Canada Pension Plan acquisition of ALLETE/Minnesota Power. Commenters include retail customers, long-term shareholders, and residents expressing concerns about private equity ownership of utilities, anticipated rate increases based on experiences in Wisconsin and Michigan, forced taxable events for shareholders, foreign ownership of essential infrastructure, and loss of local control. All commenters urge the PUC to reject the transaction.
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: 40 comments received outside deadline opposing 'BlackRock/Canada Pension Plan acquisition.' Mix of retail customers, shareholders, residents cite 'concerns about private equity ownership of utilities, anticip[ated rate increases].' Establishes template opposition arguments repeated in later batches.

docket_entries.title: Joint Coalition Comments Supporting ALJ Recommendation to Deny Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: Coalition of eight environmental and clean energy advocacy organizations submits brief comments supporting the Administrative Law Judge's recommendation to deny the acquisition. The groups reject the proposed settlement between ALLETE, the Partners, and Department of Commerce, stating 'the conditions laid out in the settlement stipulation do not sufficiently mitigate the harms and risks of the acquisition.' They explicitly support Decision Options 6 (deny the acquisition as inconsistent with public interest) and 13 (adopt ALJ Report). The filing emphasizes 'solidarity with the overwhelming number of Minnesotans that have expressed their opposition to this acquisition' and asserts 'the record shows that this proposed private equity buyout is inconsistent with the public interest.' This represents continued organized opposition from environmental advocacy community, though filing does not detail specific inadequacies in settlement conditions or present new substantive arguments beyond reference to their August 14th letter.
key_arguments: Settlement conditions do not sufficiently mitigate the harms and risks of the acquisition
The proposed private equity buyout is inconsistent with the public interest as shown by the record
Opposition reflects the overwhelming number of Minnesotans who have expressed opposition to the acquisition
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Coalition explicitly rejects the proposed settlement between ALLETE, Partners, and Department of Commerce, stating 'the condition[s are insufficient]' - marks organized opposition bloc against settlement compromise attempts.
key_excerpts: We again ask that the Commission reject the proposed settlement offered by ALLETE, Inc., the Partners, and the Department of Commerce, which is now supported by a few organizations that are not parties to the case, because the conditions laid out in the settlement stipulation do not sufficiently mitigate the harms and risks of the acquisition.

The record shows that this proposed private equity buyout is inconsistent with the public interest. In solidarity with the overwhelming number of Minnesotans that have expressed their opposition to this acquisition, we respectfully urge the Commission to deny the proposal.

docket_entries.title: Public Interest Intervenors' Joint Proposed Decision Options (Opposition Brief)
docket_entries.entry_summary: The Public Interest Intervenors (LPI, OAG, CUB, Sierra Club, CURE) filed joint proposed decision options asserting: (1) the stipulated conditions offered by Minnesota Power are 'insufficient to overcome the Administrative Law Judge's factual findings and conclusions that the Acquisition is inconsistent with the public interest'; and (2) 'there are no conditions that could be added that would overcome the Petitioners' failure to meet their burden to prove the Acquisition, as proposed, is consistent with the public interest.' They recommend the Commission adopt denial options 6, 13, and 21 from Staff briefing papers. However, 'if the Commission nonetheless approves the Acquisition over the Public Interest Intervenors' objection,' they propose five specific conditions focused on maintaining regulatory oversight. These conditions address: mandatory disclosure of all investor communications, board independence requirements (5 of 8 independent directors must be disinterested and not Partner-appointed, with 3 from Minnesota), a legally binding 12-year prohibition on selling ownership except via IPO or to regulated utility, Commission pre-approval of all affiliated interest agreements including the confidential governance document showing Partner consent rights, and enhanced affiliated interest reporting requiring disclosure of any contracts over $50,000 with entities more than 5% owned by the Partners or BlackRock. This filing represents the coalition's final position post-hearing: deny the deal, but if approved, impose these five safeguards to preserve Commission regulatory authority.
key_arguments: The stipulated decision options offered by Minnesota Power to date are insufficient to overcome the ALJ's factual findings and legal conclusions that the Acquisition is inconsistent with the public interest
There are no conditions that could be added that would overcome the Petitioners' failure to meet their burden of proving the Acquisition is consistent with the public interest

If the Commission approves despite intervenor objections, it must adopt conditions that 'ensure the Commission retains its existing authority and effective ability to continue regulating ALLETE d/b/a Minnesota Power'
The Commission needs access to all investor communications and reports provided by the Partners to maintain effective oversight of a utility owned by private equity infrastructure funds
Board independence must be preserved through requirements for disinterested directors not appointed by or under influence of the Partners, with Minnesota representation
A legally binding 12-year ownership commitment is necessary to prevent the short-term ownership cycles typical of private equity funds

All affiliated interest agreements must receive Commission pre-approval, including the confidential governance document that delineates Partner consent rights over strategic, business, and operational decisions of ALLETE

Enhanced affiliated interest transaction reporting and arm's-length certification is required given the extensive portfolio holdings of CPP Investments, GIP, and BlackRock across utility suppliers, vendors, and customers
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Argues 'the stipulated conditions offered by Minnesota Power are insufficient to overcome the Administrative Law Judge's factual findings and conclusions that the Acquisition is inconsistent' with public interest - first direct assessment that settlement conditions cannot cure ALJ's identified deficiencies.
key_excerpts: The Public Interest Intervenors respectfully assert that (i) the stipulated decision options offered to date, as summarized in the filing by Minnesota Power on September 24, 2025, are insufficient to overcome the Administrative Law Judge's factual findings and conclusions that the Acquisition is inconsistent with the public interest; and (ii) there are no conditions that could be added that would overcome the Petitioners' failure to meet their burden to prove the Acquisition, as proposed, is consistent with the public interest.

If the Commission nonetheless approves the Acquisition over the Public Interest Intervenors' objection, the Public Interest Intervenors urge the Commission to adopt the attached decision options to ensure the Commission retains its existing authority and effective ability to continue regulating ALLETE d/b/a Minnesota Power.

docket_entries.title: Sierra Club/CURE Proposed Decision Options - Conditional Opposition Framework
docket_entries.entry_summary: Sierra Club and CURE maintain their fundamental opposition to the acquisition, stating: 'The risks of the proposed acquisition outweigh any potential benefits, and the acquisition is therefore not consistent with the public interest, for the reasons explained in the Administrative Law Judge's report.' They emphasize that 'the proposed decision options offered to date, including those listed in Minnesota Power's September 24, 2025 proposed settlement stipulation, do not resolve our concerns' and that they 'do not believe the proposed acquisition could be made consistent with the public interest even if all of the proposed decision options were adopted, as those conditions would not be sufficient to mitigate the acquisition's risks.' However, recognizing the Commission may approve despite their opposition, they propose three significantly strengthened decision options that would fundamentally alter the transaction's commitments. Their proposed Decision Option G would increase the Clean Firm Technology Fund to $250 million (up from settlement terms) with accelerated deployment ($125M by March 2030, full amount by March 2035) and $16.67M biennial installments. Decision Option F would make Carbon-Free Standard compliance binding with explicit commitment that 'Partners commit to support Minnesota Power with additional expertise and capital as necessary to enable Minnesota Power to comply with the Carbon Free Standard.' Most significantly, Decision Option H would require a 'Clean Firm Plan' eliminating the 750 MW combined-cycle gas expansion from the March 2025 IRP, accelerating Boswell coal retirement (Unit 3 by end of 2029, Unit 4 by 2032), retiring Hibbard by 2030, and minimizing gas additions to 'reduce stranded asset risk.' They explicitly state their support for the joint conditions proposed by Large Power Intervenors, Attorney General, CUB, and other parties in a separate filing. This filing reveals the gap between the settlement stipulation and what major environmental intervenors would need to support approval, with implications for both deal timeline and economics.
key_arguments: The proposed acquisition is not consistent with public interest for reasons explained in the ALJ report, and existing settlement conditions (including Minnesota Power's September 24 stipulation) are insufficient to mitigate risks
Even if all proposed decision options to date were adopted, they 'would not be sufficient to mitigate the acquisition's risks,' requiring denial and adoption of Staff decision options 6, 13, and 21

If Commission approves despite opposition, the Clean Firm Technology Fund must be increased to $250 million with faster deployment ($125M by March 2030, full $250M by March 2035) to provide meaningful clean energy investment
Partners must make binding, enforceable commitment to Carbon-Free Standard compliance, not just acknowledgment, with explicit obligation to provide 'additional expertise and capital as necessary'
The 750 MW combined-cycle natural gas expansion proposed in March 2025 IRP must be eliminated to minimize stranded asset risk and avoid locking in decades of fossil fuel dependence
Coal retirement at Boswell must be accelerated beyond settlement terms: Unit 3 by end of 2029, Unit 4 by 2032, with clean firm replacement evaluated using updated externality values
Natural gas additions must be minimized across the portfolio to reduce long-term stranded asset risk for ratepayers as clean technologies become more cost-effective
Hibbard Energy Center must retire by 2030 to align with decarbonization timeline
Amended NTEC affiliated interest agreements with updated capacity dedication amounts must be filed by October 15, 2025 to allow parties to analyze whether that capacity level serves public interest
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Establishes conditional framework while maintaining core opposition: 'The risks of the proposed acquisition outweigh any potential benefits, and the acquisition is therefore not consistent with the public interest, for the reasons explained in the Administrative Law Judge's report' - suggests willingness to propose conditions despite fundamental opposition.
key_excerpts: The Clean Energy Parties continue to believe that the risks of the proposed acquisition outweigh any potential benefits, and that the acquisition is therefore not consistent with the public interest, for the reasons explained in the Administrative Law Judge's report.

The proposed decision options offered to date, including those listed in Minnesota Power's September 24, 2025 proposed settlement stipulation, do not resolve our concerns. We do not believe the proposed acquisition could be made consistent with the public interest even if all of the proposed decision options were adopted, as those conditions would not be sufficient to mitigate the acquisition's risks.

The Partners commit to support Minnesota Power with additional expertise and capital as necessary to enable Minnesota Power to comply with the Carbon Free Standard through implementation of Commission Orders. [Proposed Decision Option F]

Alloy Parent shall make $16.67 million installments every two years as part of Minnesota Power's biennial IRP filings... Alloy Parent and Minnesota Power shall complete $125 million in contributions to the fund by March 3, 2030, and shall complete all biennial contributions to the Fund totaling $250 million no later than March 3, 2035. [Proposed Decision Option G]

The Clean Firm Plan shall not include the 750 MW of new combined-cycle natural gas resources proposed in Minnesota Power's March 3, 2025 integrated resource plan, and shall minimize the size and capacity factor of natural gas resources added to minimize the risk of stranded assets for Minnesota Power customers. [Proposed Decision Option H]

The Clean Firm Plan shall evaluate an end to coal combustion at Boswell Energy Center Unit 3 by no later than the end of 2029 and an end to coal combustion at Boswell Unit 4 by no later than 2032. [Proposed Decision Option H]

docket_entries.title: Clean Energy Parties' Proposed Decision Options (Certificate of Service)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Certificate of service for the Clean Energy Parties' (Sierra Club and CURE) proposed decision options filed on September 30, 2025. This procedural filing confirms service of their substantive recommendations to the Commission on how to decide the merger. The proposed decision options themselves would contain their specific conditions or relief requested, but are not included in this certificate. This represents the Clean Energy Parties' final position statement in the Post-Decision briefing phase.
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Procedural filing only - confirms service of substantive Entry #32 recommendations. Contains no new arguments or evidence beyond certification of proper notice.

docket_entries.title: Certificate of Service - Settlement Stipulation Second Addendum
docket_entries.entry_summary: Minnesota Power files certificate of service confirming that the Second Addendum to Settlement Stipulation was properly served on all parties via eDockets and email/mail on September 30, 2025. This is a procedural filing required to document proper service and does not contain any substantive merger-related content.
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Procedural filing only - certifies proper service of Second Addendum to Settlement Stipulation via eDockets and email/mail on September 30, 2025. No substantive merger arguments.

docket_entries.title: Minnesota Power Quantifies Settlement Benefits at $183-258 Million
docket_entries.entry_summary: Minnesota Power filed its Commission-requested quantification of Settlement benefits, calculating total value at $183-258 million over varied time horizons. The filing breaks benefits into three categories: (1) Direct Customer Benefits totaling $175-250M including $25-100M rate case stay-out value (variance reflects different methodologies - DOC's $25M estimate vs. MP's $100M estimate), $50M bill credits, $50M Clean Firm Technology Fund, $10M Residential Energy Bill Mitigation Fund, $5.5M from ROE reduction to 9.65%, $29.6M flotation cost savings, and $3.5M arrears forgiveness; (2) Continued Community Investment of $7.5M; and (3) Non-quantifiable benefits including ROE/capital structure caps through 2030, service quality metrics with financial penalties, five-year employee protections (extended from two years), clean energy commitments, governance protections with majority independent board, and extensive ring-fencing. MP emphasizes: 'By any reasonable account, the financial benefits to customers are extensive' and notes the $258M figure increased from 'nearly $200 million' through the September 24 Addendum incorporating Commissioner Decision Options. The filing includes an executable spreadsheet with detailed assumptions and acknowledges 'different parties may make different assumptions' on items like rate case stay-out value.
key_arguments: Total quantifiable settlement benefits reach $183-258 million depending on assumptions, with $145-220 million delivered within five years (2026-2030)
Rate case stay-out through November 2026 delivers $25-100M in customer savings (DOC June 2025 estimate: $25M for final rates; MP September 2025 estimate: $100M for interim rates)

Direct customer benefits of $175-250M include immediate cash benefits ($50M bill credits, $10M mitigation fund, $3.5M arrears forgiveness) plus ongoing savings from ROE reduction ($5.5M over two years) and flotation cost elimination ($29.6M total value)
$50M Clean Firm Technology Fund provides dedicated resources for clean firm technology development through three $16.67M installments (2026, 2028, 2030)

Non-quantifiable benefits provide substantial additional value including ROE cap at 9.65% and capital structure cap through 2030, service quality financial penalties, extended employee protections, and extensive governance/ring-fencing protections
Settlement benefits increased by $58-60M through September 24, 2025 Addendum incorporating Commissioner Decision Options, demonstrating continued improvement of customer value
Customer benefits are enforceable through multiple tools including potential ROE penalties, extensive reporting requirements, and both Commission and court enforcement mechanisms
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Provides concrete dollar quantification of settlement benefits: '$183-258 million over varied time horizons' broken into three categories including '$25-100M rate case stay-out value.' First specific financial valuation of settlement terms in docket.
key_excerpts: By any reasonable account, the financial benefits to customers are extensive. In total, the best information available to Minnesota Power and the Partners indicates the total value of the quantifiable benefits of the Settlement was nearly $200 million, and increases to $258 million as a result of the September 24, 2025 Addendum to Settlement incorporating additional Commissioner Decision Options.

We recognize that different parties may make different assumptions. For example, the Company currently estimates that the value of a rate case stay out is $100 million, a figure which has increased over time. Other parties who typically participate in Minnesota Power's rate cases may ultimately have differing views on likely rates.

It is also important to underscore that these are the quantifiable benefits. Other commitments – such as a cap on the rate of return on equity and capital structure, or the extension of existing employee benefits – have real benefits but cannot be assigned a numeric value.

docket_entries.title: Large Power Intervenors' Proposed Decision Options - Rate Cap, Bill Credits, and Rate Case Moratorium
docket_entries.entry_summary: LPI endorses the Public Interest Intervenors' positions and proposes three strengthened conditions specifically protecting industrial customers from rate impacts. LPI emphasizes the ALJ's finding that 'projected rate increases will likely exceed the inflation rate, adversely impacting the budgets of residential customers and the economic competitiveness of ALLETE's large industrial customers.' LPI notes Minnesota Power's delivered energy costs for large power customers (10.84¢/kWh) and large light & power customers (12.06¢/kWh) are already 'well-above the national average of 8.15 cents/kWh for industrial customers' and states 'This divergence needs prompt attention by the Commission.' Their proposed conditions would: (1) lock in the 7.25% ROE cap through 2030 with penalty provisions for violations, (2) reduce bill credits from $100M to $75M but extend distribution through 2035, (3) prohibit decoupling mechanisms that would insulate MP from industrial load loss, and (4) extend the rate case moratorium two additional years to November 2028. These modifications appear designed to provide longer-term rate stability and prevent MP from shifting merger costs to industrial customers through alternative ratemaking mechanisms.
key_arguments: Minnesota Power's current rates for large industrial customers (10.84-12.06¢/kWh) already exceed the national average (8.15¢/kWh), creating competitiveness concerns that merger-related rate increases will exacerbate

ALJ found that projected rate increases from the Acquisition will likely exceed inflation, adversely impacting large industrial customers' economic competitiveness - this finding justifies stronger rate protections
Rate of return should be capped at 7.25% through 2030 with penalty mechanism (up to 100 basis point reduction) to ensure compliance with merger conditions and prevent excessive returns
Bill credits should be reduced to $75M but distributed over longer period (through 2035) to provide sustained rate relief rather than front-loaded benefit
Minnesota Power should be prohibited from proposing industrial decoupling or sales-revenue true-ups during bill credit period, preventing utility from insulating itself from load loss consequences
Rate case moratorium should extend to November 2028 (not 2026) to provide industrial customers longer period of rate stability and prevent premature recovery of merger costs
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Proposes 'three strengthened conditions specifically protecting industrial customers from rate impacts' beyond general settlement terms. Quotes ALJ finding that 'projected rate increases will likely exceed the inflation rate, adversely impacting the budgets of' industrial users—targets specific customer class protections.
key_excerpts: The Administrative Law Judge reviewed the record and determined that projected rate impacts directly resulting from the Acquisition are concerning. She stated that the evidence in the record on 'projected rate increases will likely exceed the inflation rate, adversely impacting the budgets of residential customers and the economic competitiveness of ALLETE's large industrial customers.'

According to Minnesota Power's compliance filing in its last general rate case (MPUC Docket No. 23-155), the average delivered cost of energy, including riders, for Minnesota Power's large power class and large light & power class, as of year ending 2024, is 10.84 cents/kWh and 12.06 cents/kWh, respectively. This all-in price is well-above the national average of 8.15 cents/kWh for industrial customers for the year ending 2024. This divergence needs prompt attention by the Commission.

docket_entries.title: Batch Public Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition (9 Retail Customers)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Nine Minnesota residents oppose the acquisition, expressing concerns about private equity ownership of essential services. Common themes include: (1) Fear of rate increases after any rate freeze expires ('On day one of year six, you better believe rates will begin to skyrocket'), (2) Distrust of BlackRock's profit motive over service ('An investment firm will not look out for the long term needs of our residents or environment. We can expect Black Rock to make as much money as possible, as quickly as possible, off the backs of residents'), (3) Concern about affordability during financial stress ('Many people are already struggling financially and would not have any other option for this essential service'), and (4) General opposition to private equity in utilities ('Power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center'). Several cite data center concerns. All request denial of the transaction.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership will prioritize profits over long-term needs of residents and environment, leading to rate increases after any initial rate freeze expires
Affordability concerns are critical as residents have no alternative provider for this essential service during existing financial hardship
BlackRock's business model of extracting maximum profit quickly is incompatible with utility service obligations
Essential services like power should not be owned by profit-driven investment firms or private equity
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates standard opposition themes: rate increase fears after freeze ('On day one of year six, you better believe rates will begin to skyrocket') and distrust of BlackRock/private equity ownership of essential services. No new substantive arguments beyond prior public comments.
key_excerpts: We can expect Black Rock to make as much money as possible, as quickly as possible, off the backs of residents. Minnesotans deserve a power company that we can trust to maintain affordable utility rates, especially during winter.
On day one of year six, you better believe rates will begin to skyrocket. These firms also know big data centers are worming their way into our states as well and they want to get the heads up now.
Many people are already struggling financially and would not have any other option for this essential service. Thus, this deal is against the interests of those served by Minnesota Power.

docket_entries.title: Community Coalition Letter Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.filer_name: Community Members Opposed to Sale of MN Power to BlackRock
docket_entries.entry_summary: Coalition of Minnesota community groups, faith leaders, labor members, and citizens submits letter urging Commission to deny BlackRock's acquisition of Minnesota Power. The letter characterizes the transaction as private equity extracting wealth from communities and cites harms private equity has caused in housing, healthcare, and education sectors. The coalition argues 'private equity hurts public life by consolidating financial wealth and political power into the hands of the few, skewing the one voice, one vote promise of democratic governance.' References ALJ McKenzie's recommendation to deny, stating the ALJ found the deal 'not only inconsistent with the public interest but that, if approved, the deal would result in net harm.' No specific conditions or remedies proposed - purely oppositional based on general anti-private equity principles rather than deal-specific concerns.
key_arguments: Private equity consolidates financial wealth and political power, undermining democratic governance
Private equity has harmed housing affordability, healthcare quality, and education access in other sectors
ALJ found the proposed acquisition inconsistent with public interest and would result in net harm
Utilities are essential services Minnesotans need to survive and should not be sold to private equity
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates opposition to 'private equity extracting wealth from communities' and cites general harms private equity has caused elsewhere. Coalition format adds organizational backing but arguments mirror prior public comments without new evidence or legal theory.
key_excerpts: We are sick and tired of the corporate elite extracting wealth from our people and communities and making decisions about us, without us.

The ALJ found that the proposed acquisition of ALLETE, Inc., parent company of Minnesota Power, by Global Infrastructure Partners and Canada Pension Plan Investment Board is not only inconsistent with the public interest but that, if approved, the deal would result in net harm.

docket_entries.title: Minnesota Legislators Oppose Private Equity Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: 19 Minnesota state legislators (Senators and Representatives) formally urge the PUC to reject the acquisition, stating they 'stand shoulder to shoulder with our constituents' against the 'private equity buyout.' They explicitly endorse the ALJ's findings that 'unequivocally demonstrate that the proposed acquisition is not in the public interest' and agree that 'the risks and harms would outweigh the minimal, short-term benefits.' The legislators frame approval as violating the PUC's public mission and express concern that turning essential electricity service 'over to the likes of BlackRock risks putting yet another burden on working people they simply cannot afford.' No conditions are offered; they simply urge the Commission to 'serve the public and deny the proposal.' This represents significant political pressure from elected officials representing both the service territory and broader state interests.
key_arguments: Acquisition violates PUC's mandate to 'improve the lives of all Minnesotans' and regulate with justice and reason to ensure safety, reliability and sustainability
Turning essential electricity service over to private equity like BlackRock 'risks putting yet another burden on working people they simply cannot afford' given existing economic uncertainty
ALJ findings 'unequivocally demonstrate that the proposed acquisition is not in the public interest' and risks/harms outweigh minimal, short-term benefits
Approval would violate the Commission's 'critical public mission' to serve the public interest
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Adds political/legislative weight by having elected officials 'stand shoulder to shoulder with our constituents' and formally 'endorse the ALJ's findings that unequivocally demonstrate that the prop[osal should be rejected].' Leverages ALJ authority as basis for legislative opposition—different institutional voice than prior entries.
key_excerpts: We recognize and uplift the Administrative Law Judge's findings which unequivocally demonstrate that the proposed acquisition is not in the public interest, and her recommendation that the Commission reject the proposal. Furthermore, we agree that the risks and harms would outweigh the minimal, short-term benefits.
Turning an essential service like the electricity they depend on day-to-day over to the likes of BlackRock risks putting yet another burden on working people they simply cannot afford.

docket_entries.title: Clean Water Action Minnesota - Petition to Deny Acquisition Based on Environmental Justice and PE Business Model Concerns
docket_entries.entry_summary: Clean Water Action Minnesota urges denial of the acquisition, arguing private equity ownership is 'anathema to the fundamental responsibility of a public utility, which is of course, to provide a public benefit.' They emphasize ALJ McKenzie's clear recommendation to deny and raise three core concerns: (1) Rate increases are 'all but necessitate[d]' by the 19-22% premium paid and PE's need for 15-20% annual returns, citing Michigan's UPPCO experience where PE ownership led to rates $9/kWh higher than other utilities; (2) PE ownership threatens Minnesota's net-zero emission goals because BlackRock's ownership stakes in Minnesota Power customers (9.72% of Cleveland Cliffs, 10.87% of US Steel - representing 56% of retail sales) creates vertical integration that could 'incentivize resource-intensive and environmentally-degrading activities, such as mining' and bring energy-intensive data centers that spike rates and threaten grid reliability; (3) PE's bankruptcy record (11 of 17 largest US bankruptcies in first half of 2024, including GIP's 65% stake in SunPower which filed bankruptcy in August 2024) makes it unsuitable for long-term sustainable energy provision. They conclude: 'Private equity has yet to show that their involvement does, in fact, lead to a better service for the consumer.'
key_arguments: Private equity business model conflicts with public utility responsibility: 'Private equity (PE), as you know, is responsible first to its shareholders and returning a profit to them. This is anathema to the fundamental responsibility of a public utility, which is of course, to provide a public benefit.'

The 19-22% acquisition premium and PE target returns of 15-20% per year (plus 5-7% annual cash yield) will necessitate rate increases to 150,000 Minnesotans, following Michigan UPPCO precedent where PE ownership led to rates $9/kWh higher than other investor-owned utilities

BlackRock's ownership stakes in Minnesota Power's major customers (9.72% Cleveland Cliffs, 10.87% US Steel - 56% of retail sales) creates vertical integration conflict where 'they would be paying themselves via power purchase agreements and could incentivize resource-intensive and environmentally-degrading activities' that conflict with state emission goals

PE ownership threatens state climate goals by incentivizing high-consumption customers like data centers that 'suck up our finite clean water resources, along with spiking residential electricity rates and threatening the reliability of our electrical grid'

PE bankruptcy risk makes it unsuitable for utility ownership: 'private equity firms played a role in 11 of the 17 largest US corporate bankruptcies during the first six months of 2024' and companies bought by PE are '10 times more likely to go bankrupt,' including GIP's 65% stake in SunPower which filed bankruptcy August 2024

PE ownership harms employees and local economy: studies show average job losses of 4.4% in two years after PE buyout, 13% employment shrinkage after public company buyouts, and 1.7% decrease in average worker earnings
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Introduces environmental/public benefit framing not previously emphasized: 'private equity ownership is anathema to the fundamental responsibility of a public utility, which is of course, to provide a public benefit.' Explicitly endorses ALJ McKenzie's denial recommendation and raises three core concerns (not fully detailed in summary).
key_excerpts: Private equity (PE), as you know, is responsible first to its shareholders and returning a profit to them. This is anathema to the fundamental responsibility of a public utility, which is of course, to provide a public benefit.

Administrative Law Judge Megan McKenzie could not have been more clear: '...ALLETE, Inc. and the Partners have not met their burden of proof to show the transaction is consistent with the public interest. As a result, the Commission should DENY the petition for approval of an acquisition of ALLETE by the Partners.'
As a result of the rate hikes sought by its private equity owners, UPPCO rates were $9 per kilowatt-hour (KwH) higher than the average rate at other investor-owned utilities in Michigan.

BlackRock controls 9.72% and 10.87%, respectively, of Cleveland Cliffs and US Steel, which represent 56% of Minnesota Power's retail sales from their taconite mining operations. If BlackRock were to own the energy production, it would create a situation where they would paying themselves via power purchase agreements and could incentivize resource-intensive and environmentally-degrading activities, such as mining.

Private equity has yet to show that their involvement does, in fact, lead to a better service for the consumer, but they have shown (GIP, in particular) that they know how to drive a business to bankruptcy.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 8 Retail Customer Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: Eight individual Minnesota Power customers submitted opposition comments after the comment period closed. All uniformly oppose the sale to BlackRock/private equity on grounds of rate increases, loss of local control, and concerns about profit-driven operation of essential public services. Common themes: (1) utilities are public goods that shouldn't be privately owned, (2) private equity will prioritize profits over ratepayers, (3) rates will increase, (4) renewable energy commitments may not be honored, and (5) loss of regulatory oversight and local accountability. Several cite Texas utility failures as cautionary examples. One commenter (Lori Groseth) raises shareholder concerns about tax implications and lost voting rights. Several emphasize customers have no alternative provider (captive market). One out-of-state commenter (Daena Winston from New York) warns approval would set precedent for nationwide utility acquisitions by BlackRock.
key_arguments: Utilities are public services essential for survival and should not be owned by profit-driven private companies focused on bottom line over public interest
As a regional monopoly, customers have no alternative provider and cannot 'shop around,' making them captive to whatever rates/practices the new owner imposes
Private equity ownership will inevitably lead to rate increases to recover acquisition premium and generate investor returns
Promises made now (renewable energy transition, rate commitments) can be broken once sale is complete with no recourse
Loss of local accountability and regulatory oversight compared to publicly owned utility - decisions will be driven by distant Wall Street financial interests
Risk of deferred maintenance to cut costs and boost profits, threatening reliability (citing Texas utility failures as precedent)
Shareholders will face tax consequences from forced sale and loss of voting rights in regional energy company
Approval would set dangerous nationwide precedent enabling BlackRock to acquire utilities across the country
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: REITERATIVE: Batch of late-filed customer comments echoing standard opposition themes already in docket: rate increase fears, loss of local control, profit-driven utility concerns. No novel arguments or evidence beyond cumulative weight of customer opposition voices.
key_excerpts: We do not have the option to choose our power companies, because they're operated by region. If this sale goes through, we will not have nearly the influence or oversight compared to what exists with a publicly owned utility.

Electricity is a public good, like roads and bridges and schools. What will stop Blackrock from significantly increasing the price of electricity? Why would we willingly give away public control of a public good/service to a private company?

Since BlackRock has proposed to purchase the shares at a premium, will their desire to prioritize recovery on that investment and future profits jeopardize affordable energy for ratepayers as well as the jobs for Northern Minnesotans?

docket_entries.title: MN PUC Response to Legislative Correspondence - Ex Parte Notification
docket_entries.entry_summary: The Minnesota PUC Deputy Executive Secretary filed a copy of a standardized email sent to 14 Minnesota state legislators who had submitted letters to the docket. The email thanks legislators for their correspondence, provides information about the October 3, 2025 hearing at 9:00 am, explains that the Commission cannot provide additional comment due to ex parte rules, and directs legislators to subscribe to the docket or submit additional comments if desired. This is a procedural filing to document the communication and ensure transparency in compliance with ex parte restrictions on substantive communications with parties.
key_arguments: No arguments - purely procedural notification of response to legislative correspondence
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: PROCEDURAL ONLY: PUC notifies docket that 14 state legislators received standardized response emails about October 3, 2025 hearing logistics and ex parte communication rules. No substantive positions or new arguments—purely administrative documentation.
key_excerpts: As you may know, the Commission is bound by ex-parte rules and therefore cannot provide any additional comment on your letter.

docket_entries.title: Staff Comprehensive Decision Option List with Three New Staff Alternatives
docket_entries.entry_summary: Staff presents 43+ decision options for Commission vote on October 3, 2025, organized as a decision tree: (1) approve settlement as-is (Option 4), (2) approve settlement with modifications from Options 7-43 (Option 5), or (3) deny the transaction (Option 6, supported by ALJ/OAG/intervenors). Three NEW staff alternatives appear: **Staff Alternative 5** would adopt the settlement as amended by both September addenda while allowing Commission to revisit findings of fact separately. **Staff Revised 13.A** (compromise between Resolving Parties and staff) would 'direct' rather than 'delegate authority to' Executive Secretary to modify ALJ report, ensuring findings align with Commission decisions but removing discretionary language. **Staff Revised 15** would adopt Petitioners' revised findings on commitments BUT explicitly reject findings 148A/298 (prejudging that ring-fencing will be in public interest) and findings 152/152C (affiliated interest statute interpretation). The option list shows significant tension over: (1) whether to adopt or reject ALJ report wholesale (Options 13 vs 14), (2) how much discretion Executive Secretary gets in modifying findings (multiple competing 13.A versions), (3) Commissioner Ham's three aggressive new conditions (Options 23-25) requiring ROE/ROR caps through 2030, continued SEC-style disclosures, and Department-managed independent audits - with Resolving Parties filing 'Revised' versions weakening each one (changing 'rate of return' to 'ROE', 'any and all' to 'relevant' information, Department 'managed audit' to 'opportunity to review scope'). This reveals settlement parties are still negotiating to water down Commissioner conditions up to the vote date.
key_arguments: Staff Alternative 5 presents a middle path: adopt all settlement conditions agreed in both September addenda (accepting the negotiated compromises on Options 7-12 and 23-43) but preserve Commission's ability to make independent findings of fact through separate consideration of Options 13-22

Staff Revised 13.A removes discretionary 'delegate authority' language in favor of 'direct' - accepting Resolving Parties' framing that Executive Secretary should not have discretion to modify findings beyond ensuring consistency, but staff removes 'as needed to ensure' qualifier

Staff Revised 15 would adopt Petitioners' revised commitment findings BUT explicitly exclude finding 148A/298 ('the forthcoming proposal to make the regulated utility a separate legal entity will be in the public interest') as improper predetermination, and exclude findings 152/152C on affiliated interest statute interpretation, while rejecting ALJ findings 152, 274, 275 on affiliated interest reporting requirements

Commissioner Ham's three conditions (23-25) show aggressive attempt to impose: (1) 7.25% rate-of-return cap through 2030 with 100bp penalty authority, (2) continued SEC disclosure requirements, (3) Department-managed independent audits - but Resolving Parties' revised versions systematically weaken each (ROE cap instead of ROR cap, 'relevant' information instead of 'any and all', 'opportunity to review' instead of 'managed')

The option structure reveals strategic choice architecture: Option 4 (approve as-is) is clean but likely insufficient for Commissioners given extensive modifications proposed; Option 5 (approve with modifications) opens door to 35+ sub-options creating negotiation complexity; Option 6 (deny) has ALJ/OAG/all opposing intervenors support but appears unlikely given settlement momentum
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Staff introduces structured decision tree with 43+ options and three new staff-originated alternatives (not described in summary). Frames Commission's binary choice: approve settlement as-is (Option 4), approve with modifications (Option 5, drawing from Options 7-43), or deny (Option 6, aligned with ALJ/OAG/intervenors). Provides procedural architecture for vote.
key_excerpts: Staff Alternative 5. Approve Minnesota Power's Petition subject to the terms of the July 11, 2025 Settlement Stipulation, as amended by the September 24, 2025 Addendum to Settlement Stipulation and the September 30, 2025 Second Addendum to Settlement Stipulation, except for the recommendations on Decision Options 13–20 regarding findings of fact. [Staff note: Staff Alternative 5 would adopt all conditions agreed to by the Resolving Parties in their amended settlement stipulation from the ranges of Decision Options 7–12 and 23–43. If this option is selected, Commissioners should also consider Decision Options 13–22 relating to findings of fact and other Commission action, but need not revisit 7–12 or 23–43.]

Staff Revised 15. Adopt the modifications to ALJ Report Section III.C (findings 139–178) and new Section III.CC (findings 284–325), regarding Petitioners' proposed commitments, as shown in Attachment B to the Petitioners' August 4, 2025 Exceptions, to the extent consistent with the Commission's decisions. Do not adopt Petitioners' proposed findings 148A and 298 to the extent that they imply a predetermination that the forthcoming proposal to make the regulated utility a separate legal entity will be in the public interest. Do not adopt Petitioners' proposed findings 152 and 152C related to interpretation of the affiliated interest statute.

Tuma Revised Resolving Parties Revised Ham 23. Minnesota Power's rate of return on equity is capped at 9.78 percent (as approved in Docket No. E-015/GR-23-155) and Minnesota Power's equity ratio in its capital structure will be capped at 53.0 percent until December 31st, 2030. This condition does not prohibit Minnesota Power from filing new rate cases or from proposing in rate cases what an ROE would be absent the temporary cap. The Commission may reduce the rate of return on equity by up to 100 basis points if it finds any significant violation of conditions imposed in this docket in addition to all other enforcement authorities granted in the Settlement Stipulation and Addendum and within the statutory authority of the Commission.

docket_entries.title: Compiled Settlement Decision Options - Resolving Parties' Consolidated Agreement
docket_entries.entry_summary: Staff has compiled the comprehensive settlement agreement reflecting negotiations through three sequential documents (July 11 Settlement Stipulation, September 24 Addendum, September 30 Second Addendum).
