The filing explicitly states: 'This list compiles the decision options agreed to by ALLETE d/b/a Minnesota Power, Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, Global Infrastructure Management, the Minnesota Department of Commerce, Energy CENTS Coalition, IBEW Local 31, LIUNA Minnesota and North Dakota, North Central States Regional Council of Carpenters, the International Union of Operating Engineers Local 49, and other participants (collectively the Resolving Parties).' Critical provisions include: (1) ROE and equity caps through 2030 with penalty provisions allowing 100 basis point ROE reduction for significant violations; (2) Enhanced regulatory oversight through quarterly FTE reporting, Department of Commerce audit review authority, and continued SEC-level disclosure requirements despite going private; (3) Substantial ratepayer benefits including $50 million in bill credits (reduced from initially proposed $75 million) and $10 million energy bill mitigation fund for income-qualified customers; (4) Strong labor protections maintaining staffing levels within 10% of historic averages (1,078 employees), protecting call center jobs in Minnesota (22+ employees), and extending nonunion compensation protections to 5 years; (5) Accelerated clean energy transition requiring Clean Firm Plan within 90 days that minimizes natural gas resources and contemplates Boswell interconnection replacement. The document notes Staff Alternative Decision Option 5 incorporates all listed conditions, suggesting staff support for the comprehensive package. Multiple conditions require 30-day compliance filings with Executive Secretary approval authority, indicating streamlined implementation. The settlement represents resolution among all major intervenors including labor, consumer advocates, and the Department of Commerce.
key_arguments: The settlement provides sufficient ratepayer protections through ROE/equity caps, enhanced regulatory oversight (SEC-level disclosures, audit review, quarterly reporting), and penalty provisions allowing up to 100 basis point ROE reduction for condition violations

Labor concerns are adequately addressed through minimum staffing requirements (10% of 1,078 historic average), Minnesota call center job protections (22+ employees for 10 years), 5-year compensation protections for nonunion employees, and restrictions on FTE reductions or relocations without Commission approval

Ratepayer benefits are substantial with $50 million in bill credits by 2032, $10 million energy bill mitigation fund for income-qualified customers, and continued residential arrears payment commitments

The Clean Firm Plan requirement accelerates clean energy transition by requiring filing within 90 days that minimizes natural gas resources, contemplates Boswell interconnection replacement, and incorporates $50 million Clean Firm Fund

Enhanced transparency requirements including quarterly FTE and capital plan reporting, Department of Commerce audit review authority, and continued SEC-level disclosures address concerns about going-private transaction

The settlement balances multiple stakeholder interests as demonstrated by agreement among diverse parties including unions (IBEW, LIUNA, Operating Engineers, Carpenters), consumer advocates (Energy CENTS), and Department of Commerce
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: COMPILATION: Consolidates evolving settlement agreement reflecting negotiations across three dated iterations. Appears to be administrative consolidation of terms already introduced in prior entries (#34, #35, #37, #38 are settlement-related filings), not introducing substantively new conditions beyond what parties negotiated.
key_excerpts: The Commission may reduce the rate of return on equity by up to 100 basis points if it finds any significant violation of conditions imposed in this docket in addition to all other enforcement authorities granted in the Settlement Stipulation and Addendum and within the statutory authority of the Commission.

The Company may not reduce the identified FTE complement for each business area identified in Decision Option 39.A by more than 10 percent below Minnesota Power's 5-year historic average employment (as of the date of the Acquisition) or move the primary work location out of Minnesota outside of a rate case without prior Commission approval.

Within 90 days of the Order in this docket, MP shall file an alternative resource plan scenario, henceforth called the 'Clean Firm Plan,' that takes into account modifications made in this docket and accounts for Minn. Stat. § 216B.1691, subd. 2g (the Carbon-Free Standard) in the most cost-effective manner possible... The Clean Firm Plan shall... Be developed without the 750 MW of new combined-cycle natural gas resources proposed in Minnesota Power's IRP Docket while minimizing the risk of stranded assets.

docket_entries.title: LPI Response to Minnesota Power Supplemental Filing - Requests Premium Sharing Condition
docket_entries.entry_summary: LPI responds to Minnesota Power's September 30 supplemental filing that attempts to quantify stipulation benefits. LPI urges the Commission to give 'no weight to these untested figures' and argues the stipulations 'do not address the risks documented in the record but instead amount to fringe benefits.' LPI highlights that the Administrative Law Judge found Minnesota Power and the Partners lacked credibility, that the Partners intend to earn exorbitant returns ([REDACTED] on investment vs. 8% for average risk companies), and that alleged benefits 'pale in comparison to both the premium of roughly $1.5 billion and overall price paid.' LPI emphasizes: 'If Minnesota Power and the Partners were sincere in wanting to alleviate risks and protect customers, they would have brought protections forward in their original petition or during the contested case process. Instead, after a highly contentious and litigious evidentiary process that involved numerous instances of improperly redacted documents, they navigated a stipulation with nonparties, most of which are not consumer advocates.' LPI maintains outright denial is preferable but proposes a novel fallback condition: require the Partners to demonstrate stipulation benefits were delivered AND share 50% of the premium they receive when they ultimately sell ALLETE back with ratepayers. This directly addresses the concern that 'investor and ratepayer benefits' are dramatically imbalanced. LPI also reiterates support for their September 30 decision options improving regulatory transparency and ratepayer advocate access to information.
key_arguments: The stipulations negotiated on the eve of hearings 'do not address the risks documented in the record but instead amount to fringe benefits that are not meaningful and in no way alleviate the risks presented in selling the state's assets to private equity conglomerates'

Minnesota Power and Partners lack sincerity - if they wanted to protect customers, 'they would have brought protections forward in their original petition or during the contested case process' instead of navigating last-minute stipulations 'with nonparties, most of which are not consumer advocates'

The Administrative Law Judge found Minnesota Power and Partners lacked credibility on multiple grounds, including that 'the Partners intend to earn exorbitant returns at ratepayers' expense' and 'alleged benefits pale in comparison to both the premium of roughly $1.5 billion and overall price paid'
Partners expect to earn [REDACTED] returns 'which exceed credible estimates from respectable cost of equity sources for average risk publicly traded companies, most of which are below 8 percent'

Effective regulation will be impossible because 'the information required for effective regulation will not be public or available—if ratepayer advocates don't have access to relevant information, they will be unable to help develop the record'
The dramatic imbalance between 'investor and ratepayer benefits' requires the Partners to share 50% of the premium they receive in their ultimate sale of ALLETE with ratepayers
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Direct attack on Minnesota Power's quantified benefits claims, arguing 'no weight to these untested figures' and characterizing stipulations as 'fringe benefits' that fail to 'address the risks documented in the record.' Challenges the evidentiary validity of last-minute benefit calculations.
key_excerpts: LPI is generally concerned that the stipulations do not address the risks documented in the record but instead amount to fringe benefits that are not meaningful and in no way alleviate the risks presented in selling the state's assets to private equity conglomerates.

If Minnesota Power and the Partners were sincere in wanting to alleviate risks and protect customers, they would have brought protections forward in their original petition or during the contested case process. Instead, after a highly contentious and litigious evidentiary process that involved numerous instances of improperly redacted documents, they navigated a stipulation with nonparties, most of which are not consumer advocates.

The Administrative Law Judge found that Minnesota Power and the Partners lacked credibility, that the Partners intend to earn exorbitant returns at ratepayers' expense, and that the alleged benefits pale in comparison to both the premium of roughly $1.5 billion and overall price paid by the Partners for ALLETE.

To be clear, LPI believes the Acquisition should be denied and no condition or stipulation can alleviate the risks associated therewith. Nor does LPI believe the Commission can effectively regulate the Partners because the information required for effective regulation will not be public or available.

Given the dramatic difference in investor and ratepayer benefits LPI respectfully requests that any order approving the Acquisition require the Partners to share the premium they receive in their ultimate sale of ALLETE.

docket_entries.title: Resolving Parties Reject Opposition Decision Options
docket_entries.entry_summary: The coalition of thirteen Resolving Parties (applicants plus settling intervenors) filed through Minnesota Power to reject all three decision option sets proposed by opposing parties on September 30, 2025. The Resolving Parties state they 'do not find these Decision Options to be necessary and/or reasonable' and continue to recommend approval with only the conditions in their formal Settlement and Addenda. This filing draws a clear line between the settlement coalition and remaining opposition, indicating the settling parties believe their negotiated conditions are sufficient and will not accept more stringent requirements proposed by non-settling intervenors. The filing suggests a hardened position - the Resolving Parties view the settlement as complete and are not open to incorporating additional conditions from opponents.
key_arguments: The three sets of Decision Options proposed by opposing parties on September 30, 2025 are neither necessary nor reasonable
The Decision Options in the formal Settlement and Addenda are sufficient and appropriate for approval
The Commission should approve the transaction under the Settlement terms without adopting additional conditions from non-settling intervenors
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Unified rejection by settling parties of all three decision option sets from opposing parties. States they 'do not find these Decision Options to be necessary and/or reasonable,' consolidating support behind existing stipulation framework rather than opposition alternatives.
key_excerpts: The Resolving Parties do not find these Decision Options to be necessary and/or reasonable and continue to recommend approval of the Acquisition with the Decision Options set forth in the formal Settlement and Addenda.

docket_entries.title: Sierra Club and CURE Correction to Staff's Decision Options List
docket_entries.entry_summary: Sierra Club and CURE notify the Commission of two transcription errors in Staff's October 2, 2025 Comprehensive Decision Option List. First, a complete sentence was omitted from their Decision Option H - the missing sentence requires 'By December 15, 2025, Minnesota Power shall file the Clean Firm Plan in the IRP docket, accompanied by a qualitative description of the scenario and its costs and benefits, as well as a quantitative analysis of how the scenario performs on the factors listed in Minnesota Rules part 7843.0500, subpart 3.' Second, Decision Option G is missing a redline showing that settlement language was changed from '$250 million in funds provided by Alloy Parent' to '$2 million' - a significant numerical change. The filing is procedural housekeeping to ensure the official record accurately reflects the settled parties' proposed conditions.
key_arguments: Staff's compilation omitted complete sentence from Decision Option H requiring Minnesota Power to file Clean Firm Plan by December 15, 2025 with specific analytical requirements under Minnesota Rules 7843.0500
Staff's compilation failed to show redline in Decision Option G indicating change from $250 million to $2 million in Alloy Parent funding commitment - a material 125-fold reduction
Corrections are necessary to ensure Commission's official record accurately reflects the settlement terms agreed to by the Clean Energy Parties
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Technical correction identifying transcription errors in official decision options. Restores omitted requirement: 'By December 15, 2025, Minnesota Power shall file the Clean Firm Plan' in their Decision Option H. Ensures accurate representation of their proposed conditions.
key_excerpts: The third sentence of that decision option (as filed by the Clean Energy Parties on September 30, 2025) reads: 'By December 15, 2025, Minnesota Power shall file the Clean Firm Plan in the IRP docket, accompanied by a qualitative description of the scenario and its costs and benefits, as well as a quantitative analysis of how the scenario performs on the factors listed in Minnesota Rules part 7843.0500, subpart 3.'

In the second sentence of Sierra Club and CURE's Decision Option G, the number '2' in the phrase 'using $250 million in funds provided by Alloy Parent' should be redlined, as this represents a change from the original language of Settlement Stipulation ¶ 1.63.

docket_entries.title: Joint Intervenors Object to Last-Minute $258M Benefits Claim, Maintain Opposition
docket_entries.entry_summary: CUB, OAG, Sierra Club, and CURE jointly reject Minnesota Power's September 30 filing claiming $183-258 million in customer benefits, filed just three days before the Commission's final decision. The intervenors argue: 'The evidentiary record developed over the seven-month contested case period does not support the Company's quantification of purported benefits.' Their core objection is to the largest claimed benefit - up to $100 million from deferring a rate case until November 2026: 'The Company has not filed a rate case demonstrating that this is an appropriate quantification of increases to its outstanding revenue requirement, nor has any party to this proceeding had the opportunity to review, issue discovery around, or meaningfully contest in their own filings this $100 million figure.' They argue even $258M 'pales in comparison to the $6.2 billion Acquisition, which the ALJ found includes a premium of approximately $1.5 billion over ALLETE's book value, which will accrue to the Company's shareholders.' The letter maintains that 'the evidentiary record built through the contested case process and summarized in the Administrative Law Judge's report demonstrates numerous risks that arise as a result of the Acquisition. It is not possible to assign a monetary value to these risks.' While they 'appreciate the Commissioners' efforts to propose decision options that help mitigate risk,' they oppose approval but conditionally state: 'if the Commission approves the Acquisition over our objections, we reiterate the recommendations included in the Joint Intervenors' Proposed Decision Options filed on September 30, 2025.' This indicates they have proposed fallback conditions if approval occurs, maintaining a conditional opposition stance.
key_arguments: Minnesota Power's $258 million benefits claim lacks evidentiary support and was filed only 3 days before final decision, denying parties opportunity for discovery or meaningful contest
The largest claimed benefit - $100 million from rate case deferral to November 2026 - has no supporting rate case filing or evidence demonstrating it reflects actual revenue requirement increases
Even if $258M in benefits were proven, it is dwarfed by the $6.2 billion acquisition price including $1.5 billion premium over book value accruing to shareholders
The contested case record developed over seven months demonstrates numerous risks to ratepayers that cannot be quantified monetarily
Basing approval on unsupported last-minute benefits assertions would excuse Petitioners from meeting their burden of proof that acquisition is in the public interest
If approved despite objections, effective regulation of the Partners' control over ALLETE and influence over Minnesota Power will be critical
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Joint opposition to '$183-258 million in customer benefits' claim filed just before decision. Argues 'evidentiary record developed over the seven-month contested case period does not support' these figures, challenging both timing and substance of late-stage quantified benefits assertion.
key_excerpts: The evidentiary record developed over the seven-month contested case period does not support the Company's quantification of purported benefits of the Acquisition, which it has now offered three days before the Commission makes its final decision.

The Company has not filed a rate case demonstrating that this is an appropriate quantification of increases to its outstanding revenue requirement, nor has any party to this proceeding had the opportunity to review, issue discovery around, or meaningfully contest in their own filings this $100 million figure.

Even a $258 million benefit pales in comparison to the $6.2 billion Acquisition, which the ALJ found includes a premium of approximately $1.5 billion over ALLETE's book value, which will accrue to the Company's shareholders.

Basing a decision to approve the Acquisition on an unsupported assertion that doing so would result in up to $258 million in financial benefits to customers—and relying on that unsupported assertion as outweighing the actual evidence of risks to ratepayers developed in the contested case and summarized in the ALJ Report—would excuse the Petitioners from meeting their burden of proof to show that approving the acquisition is consistent with the public interest.

docket_entries.title: Commissioner Ham Proposed Motion - Approval with Conditions
docket_entries.entry_summary: Commissioner Ham proposes to approve the ALLETE acquisition subject to the Settlement Stipulation and its two addenda, while making significant modifications to the ALJ Report to remove or revise findings critical of the transaction. The motion adopts 'Staff Alternative 5' which approves the petition subject to settlement terms. Crucially, it proposes to reject the ALJ's finding 'that there is credible evidence of potential labor risks arising from private equity acquisitions, including the possibility of layoffs or erosion of worker protections' (Decision Option 20). It also rejects the ALJ's suggestion that community supporters 'felt obligated to support Minnesota Power due to the financial support they are provided by the Company.' On capital needs, the motion modifies findings to state 'the record does not show that the alternatives proposed by intervenors could adequately meet ALLETE's anticipated capital needs' and that such alternatives 'would expose ratepayers and stakeholders to unacceptable risks while jeopardizing the utility's ability to meet legal requirements including the Carbon Free Standard.' The motion rejects ALJ findings on affiliated interest reporting concerns and incentives to grow rate base. It clarifies that CARE program commitments benefit the public because 'rate-recoverability of affordability programs alone does not guarantee that utilities will support raising surcharges to fund their expansion.' This represents a comprehensive reframing of the ALJ Report's more skeptical findings to support approval.
key_arguments: The Settlement Stipulation as amended by two addenda provides adequate conditions for approval of the acquisition

The record does not support intervenor-proposed alternatives: 'the record does not show that the alternatives proposed by intervenors could adequately meet ALLETE's anticipated capital needs' and such measures 'would expose ratepayers and stakeholders to unacceptable risks while jeopardizing the utility's ability to meet legal requirements including the Carbon Free Standard'

Access to capital justifies the transaction: 'access to capital at the scale ALLETE has forecasted it will need to meet the Carbon Free Standard would be high risk; therefore, it is unlikely the Company would be able to meet its capital needs through public markets without the Acquisition'

Labor concerns from private equity ownership lack merit: Proposes to reject ALJ finding 'that there is credible evidence of potential labor risks arising from private equity acquisitions, including the possibility of layoffs or erosion of worker protections'

CARE program commitments provide public benefit beyond rate recovery: 'rate-recoverability of affordability programs alone does not guarantee that utilities will support raising surcharges to fund their expansion' and 'there is public benefit in having an infusion of funds into affordability programming that comes from shareholders and not from other ratepayers'
Community support was genuine: Rejects ALJ suggestion that supporters 'felt obligated to support Minnesota Power due to the financial support they are provided by the Company'

Proposed governance structure adequately protects against affiliate abuse: 'the proposed governance structure balances the interests of GIP investors against the interests of CPP investors, which will reduce the risk of either partner engaging in improper affiliate transactions'

Private equity impacts vary by circumstances: Study cited by intervenors 'found that the impacts of private equity ownership vary based on factors including characteristics of the operating company and economic circumstances'
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: First Commissioner motion on record. Proposes approval with Settlement Stipulation and addenda while 'making significant modifications to the ALJ Report to remove or revise findings critical of the transaction.' Adopts Staff Alternative 5 framework, establishing specific decision path.
key_excerpts: Approve Minnesota Power's Petition subject to the terms of the July 11, 2025 Settlement Stipulation, as amended by the September 24, 2025 Addendum to Settlement Stipulation and the September 30, 2025 Second Addendum to Settlement Stipulation
Reject the ALJ's finding that there is credible evidence of potential labor risks arising from private equity acquisitions, including the possibility of layoffs or erosion of worker protections.

Modify finding 124 to add that the record does not show that the alternatives proposed by intervenors could adequately meet ALLETE's anticipated capital needs. Also find there is evidence that such measures would expose ratepayers and stakeholders to unacceptable risks while jeopardizing the utility's ability to meet legal requirements including the Carbon Free Standard.

Modify findings 133 and 135 to find that access to capital at the scale ALLETE has forecasted it will need to meet the Carbon Free Standard would be high risk; therefore, it is unlikely the Company would be able to meet its capital needs through public markets without the Acquisition.

Reject the statement in ALJ Report Addendum A, ¶ 4, that 'It is unclear whether [commenters in support of the Acquisition] felt obligated to support Minnesota Power due to the financial support they are provided by the Company.'

docket_entries.title: Public Comment Opposing BlackRock Acquisition on Principle
docket_entries.entry_summary: Stevens opposes the transaction on ideological grounds, arguing that 'power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder.' He asserts that power is 'a basic essential service that should only be able to be legally owned by the residents it serves and controlled by publicly elected individuals.' His opposition is categorical: 'Keep private equity and billionaires out of our essential services.' He requests outright denial and indicates intent to contact state representatives about legislation making private ownership of utilities illegal. This represents principled opposition to the ownership structure itself rather than specific concerns about this transaction's effects.
key_arguments: Power utilities should only be owned by residents they serve and controlled by publicly elected individuals, not private entities
Essential services like electricity should not be treated as commodities or profit centers available to the highest bidder
Private equity and billionaires should be prohibited from owning public utilities as a matter of policy
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Presents ideological opposition framework: 'power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder.' Asserts power is 'a basic essential service that should only be able to be legally owned by the residents.' Same argument as batch filings #11-13.
key_excerpts: Power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder. Power is a basic essential service that should only be able to be legally owned by the residents it serves and controlled by publicly elected individuals.
Keep private equity and billionaires out of our essential services. I am asking you to do your jobs and do what is the only moral choice, deny the sale.

docket_entries.title: Public Comment - Resident Opposition to Private Equity Ownership
docket_entries.entry_summary: Duluth resident opposes BlackRock's acquisition on ideological grounds that 'power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit-center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder.' Argues essential services should be publicly owned rather than owned by 'private equity and billionaires.' No specific conditions or remedies proposed - opposition is based on fundamental objection to private ownership structure rather than deal-specific concerns.
key_arguments: Power utilities are essential services that should be owned by the people they serve, not treated as commodities or profit centers
Private equity and billionaires should be kept out of essential services
Utility ownership should not go to the highest bidder
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates Stevens' exact language: 'power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit-center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder.' Argues essential services should be publicly owned rather than owned by 'private equity and billionaires.' No new substance beyond #14.
key_excerpts: Power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit-center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder. Power is a basic essential service that should be owned by the people who are served by it.
Keep private equity and billionaires out of our essential services.

docket_entries.title: Post-Decision Opposition - Asher Holloman Demands Accountability and Transparency
docket_entries.entry_summary: Duluth resident Asher Holloman submitted comments both before and after the Commission's approval decision, expressing fundamental opposition to 'letting BlackRock acquire MN Power.' His core argument is ideological: 'Power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder. Power is a basic essential service that should be owned by the people who are served by it. Keep private equity and billionaires out of our essential services.' Post-decision, Holloman demands transparency, noting he 'sat there and listened to each commission member explain how in just one week, all their skepticism and fears had been erased' and now has 'faith in trust in BlackRock to put the interests of Northern Minnesotans above the interests of Wall Street.' He raises accountability concerns: how will a 5-member Commission control a company with '$10 trillion in assets'? He notes 'People up here are very upset at this decision' and demands the Commission 'issue a statement explaining in detail how this decision was made and why including how it benefits us and the commitments that BlackRock has agreed to.'
key_arguments: Power utilities should not be owned by Wall Street or private equity - 'Power is a basic essential service that should be owned by the people who are served by it'
Commission underwent unexplained reversal - 'in just one week, all your skepticism and fears had been erased'
Enforcement concerns - How will 5-member MNPUC control company with $10 trillion in assets
Carbon-free goals accountability - How will Commission hold BlackRock accountable for Minnesota's 2040 carbon-free commitments
Broader trend concern - BlackRock making another utility bid; 'Does the commission think that this new trend of Wall Street owning our utilities will result in a positive outcome?'
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Same core argument as #11, #13-15: 'Power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center, and they are no[t for sale].' Notable for timing—submitted comments spanning the decision, showing opposition persisted post-approval.
key_excerpts: Power utilities are not a commodity, they are not a profit center, and they are not for sale to the highest bidder. Power is a basic essential service that should be owned by the people who are served by it. Keep private equity and billionaires out of our essential services.

I sat there and listened to each of you explain how in just one week, all your skepticism and fears had been erased and that they now had faith in trust in BlackRock to put the interests of Northern Minnesotans above the interests of Wall Street and their investors.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 13 Public Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition (Post-Decision)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Thirteen Minnesota residents submitted public comments on October 1-3, 2025 opposing the BlackRock acquisition, mostly after the PUC had already approved it on October 3. The comments express common themes: (1) distrust of private equity ownership of public utilities, (2) concerns about rate increases ('up to 267%' cited by one commenter referencing Bloomberg), (3) opposition to outside/non-local control of Minnesota utilities, (4) concerns about prioritizing AI/data center interests over residential customers, and (5) environmental concerns about Lake Superior water resources. Several commenters reference Judge McKenzie's conclusion that the sale 'would result in net harm to the public interest' and express dismay that the PUC approved despite public opposition. One commenter (Heidi Koehnle) criticizes the PUC's press release as 'PR manipulation' and notes already paying over $2,000/year to Xcel. These are typical retail customer objections with no specific conditions proposed and no legal/procedural arguments. Filed outside comment period with no impact on approved transaction.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership will prioritize profits over ratepayers and lead to significant rate increases (one commenter cites Bloomberg reporting 267% increases in areas where BlackRock bought utilities)
BlackRock will prioritize AI company/data center infrastructure over residential customer needs and exploit Lake Superior water resources for data center cooling
Non-local ownership erodes community connection and local accountability that makes utilities like Minnesota Power effective
Judge McKenzie already concluded the sale 'would result in net harm to the public interest' and PUC ignored this finding and public opposition
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Expands on #11's themes with two specific concerns: '(1) distrust of private equity ownership of public utilities, (2) concerns about rate increas[es].' Thirteen voices amplifying same ideological opposition but filed after PUC already approved transaction on October 3.
key_excerpts: Between BlackRock and data centers, people are seeing energy rate increases of up to 267% according to Bloomberg and other sources. Minnesotans are already struggling. We cannot afford this rate hike.
As Judge McKenzie concluded, the sale 'would result in net harm to the public interest.' There are no benefits to Minnesota citizens.

BlackRock in partnership with AI developers have unfair leverage to exploit the natural resources of Lake Superior and other fresh water resources, which are necessary to operate data centers. I have zero faith this is not top priority for BlackRock.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 13 Retail Customer Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition (Late Filed)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Thirteen Minnesota residents filed opposition comments on October 3-6, 2025, apparently unaware the Commission had already approved the transaction on October 3rd. The comments express anger at private equity ownership of utilities, distrust of BlackRock, and demand the Commission 'block' or 'reject' the acquisition. Common themes include: (1) fear of rate increases ('My energy rates are already going up with Xcel as my provider, under Blackrock, they will soar'), (2) environmental concerns ('not a soul can convince a single Minnesotan that Blackrock will maintain our fierce environmental standards'), (3) accountability concerns ('How will the 5 member committee that is MNPUC be able to control a company with over $10 trillion in assets?'), (4) procedural objections citing the ALJ recommendation to deny and AG Ellison's opposition, and (5) general opposition to Wall Street/private equity ownership of utilities ('Privatized profits, socialized costs'). One commenter notes 'Duluth is incredibly disappointed... Non profits, citizens, judges, and the AG warned against this... So how was this a unanimous decision?' Multiple commenters demand the Commission 'issue a statement explaining in detail how this decision was made' and how it benefits ratepayers. These are emotional, policy-based objections rather than legal arguments.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership prioritizes investor profits over ratepayer interests and will lead to rate increases: 'This acquisition prioritizes the interests of investors who have no connection to Minnesota and risks price hikes and the reliability of access to power for Minnesota residents'

BlackRock cannot be held accountable by the Commission: 'How will the 5 member committee that is MNPUC be able to control a company with over $10 trillion in assets and resources far beyond what MN as whole has?'
The deal threatens Minnesota's environmental and clean energy commitments: 'How will MNPUC hold BlackRock accountable for meeting Minnesotas 2040 carbon free goals?'
The Commission ignored the ALJ recommendation to deny, the AG's opposition, and public input: 'Non profits, citizens, judges, and the AG warned against this... So how was this a unanimous decision?'

Wall Street/private equity ownership of utilities is fundamentally contrary to Minnesota values and the public interest: 'We stand out as a state that protects the public interest over profits... Blackrock is as anti-Minnesotan as a corporation could get'
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Establishes template for subsequent opposition (#12-15): 'anger at private equity ownership of utilities, distrust of BlackRock, and demand the Commission 'block' or 'reject' [the transaction].' All filed after October 3 approval, representing coordinated but belated public opposition campaign.
key_excerpts: How have you been able to justify this decision allowing BlackRock to acquire Allete? This is disgraceful and degrades the trust that the public places into public institutions. Private equity's profit incentive structures care not about the needs of the people. It cares about turning profit... Privatized profits, socialized costs.

I sat there and listened to each of you explain how in just one week, all your skepticism and fears had been erased and that they now had faith in trust in BlackRock to put the interests of Northern Minnesotans above the interests of Wall Street and their investors.

This decision is a betrayal of Minnesotans and a clear signal that the voices of communities and ratepayers were ignored in favor of money and corporate interests... By handing control to a global investment firm with no ties to Minnesota, the Commission has effectively sold out our communities, opening the door to higher rates, reduced accountability, and decisions made by corporate executives who have no stake in our future.

docket_entries.title: Public Comment Opposing BlackRock Acquisition - Privatization Concerns
docket_entries.entry_summary: Individual commenter opposes the acquisition on ideological grounds, expressing concern about 'one of our most critical utilities from being sold to a private equity firm, particularly one with such a controversial reputation.' Opposes based on general fears about privatization of electricity and water resources, and speculative concerns about data center development. Filed outside the comment period and forwarded through Commissioner Partridge to General Counsel. No specific facts about the transaction, no citation of regulatory standards, and no specific conditions that would address concerns.
key_arguments: Opposition to sale of critical utility to private equity firm based on BlackRock's 'controversial reputation'
Concern that acquisition could lead to privatization of electricity and water resources
Speculative concern about potential data center development and privacy/security implications
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: First intervenor opposition based on privatization ideology. Argues utility should not be 'sold to a private equity firm, particularly one with such a controversial reputation.' Focuses on broader fears about 'privatization of electricity' rather than specific transaction harms.
key_excerpts: I believe it is imperative that we prevent one of our most critical utilities from being sold to a private equity firm, particularly one with such a controversial reputation.
The potential consequences are dire, as they could lead to the privatization of our electricity and eventually our water resources.

docket_entries.title: Public Comment - Robert Lyman Opposes Sale to Blackrock
docket_entries.entry_summary: Robert Lyman opposes the sale, arguing that 'it is not in the best interests of Minnesota to allow this sale to proceed.' He contends that while short-term PUC conditions are helpful, there are 'no guarantees long term, both for consumer costs and for environmental costs,' amounting to 'a net harm for the public interest.' He predicts 'a rate raise is surely in the future, beyond what Minnesota Power would have done.'
key_arguments: Short-term PUC conditions are insufficient because there are no long-term guarantees for consumer costs or environmental costs
Transaction amounts to a net harm for the public interest
Rate increases beyond what Minnesota Power would have imposed are inevitable in the future
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates privatization concerns from #9 with long-term uncertainty angle: 'no guarantees long term, both for consumer costs and for environmental costs' resulting in 'net harm.' Acknowledges short-term PUC conditions but questions their durability. Similar ideological opposition without specific evidence.
key_excerpts: While the short term conditions mandated by the PUC are helpful, there are no guarantees long term, both for consumer costs and for environmental costs.
It amounts to a net harm for the public interest. A rate raise is surely in the future, beyond what Minnesota Power would have done.

docket_entries.title: Minnesota Power ROE Compliance Filing - Tariff Updates
docket_entries.entry_summary: Minnesota Power submits compliance filing per Decision Option 7.A. to implement the ROE reduction from 9.78% to 9.65% agreed to in the Settlement Stipulation (Item 1.14). The Commission voted unanimously to approve the acquisition on October 3, 2025, subject to Staff Alternative 5 which incorporated all conditions in the amended Settlement. The new 9.65% ROE will take effect 'the first full month after both of the following have occurred: (i) the close of the Acquisition and (ii) when the order of the Commission becomes final.' The ROE will remain at 9.65% until Minnesota Power's next rate case and will be used to set interim rates in that case, though the Settlement makes no commitment on ROE methodology or level for future rate cases. Minnesota Power includes amended tariff pages showing rate reductions across all customer classes and a customer notice bill insert (reviewed by Consumer Affairs Office). Implementation requires approximately 14 days for printing/insertion of notices once Commission approval is received.
key_arguments: Filing complies with Decision Option 7.A. requirement to file amended tariff with updated rate calculations and customer notice within 10 days of order
Updated Financial Schedules 1-18 support the ROE adjustment calculations
Customer notice has been coordinated with Commission's Consumer Affairs Office
New rates will begin first day of month after both acquisition close and Commission order becomes final
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Commission unanimously approved acquisition subject to Staff Alternative 5 with Settlement Stipulation. ROE reduced from 9.78% to 9.65% per Item 1.14. Establishes post-approval implementation phase with specific tariff compliance requirements per Decision Option 7.A.
key_excerpts: As a negotiated resolution, and without agreeing to any party's underlying position on this issue, Minnesota Power's currently approved Return on Equity ('ROE') will be changed from 9.78 percent to 9.65 percent. The change in ROE will take effect the first full month after both of the following have occurred: (i) the close of the Acquisition and (ii) when the order of the Commission becomes final.

The 9.65 percent ROE will remain in effect until Minnesota Power files its next rate case and will be used to set interim rates in Minnesota Power's next Minnesota rate case. Nothing in this settlement stipulation shall be considered a commitment (i) to any specific ROE in the company's next rate case or (ii) to use a specific methodology to determine the ROE in the company's next rate case.

docket_entries.title: Minnesota Power ROE Compliance Filing (Trade Secret)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Minnesota Power submitted a confidential compliance filing related to return on equity calculations or commitments. The document is designated as trade secret and not publicly available. This appears to be routine compliance with financial reporting requirements established in merger conditions or regulatory orders, rather than a substantive filing affecting deal approval or terms.
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Trade secret ROE calculation filing. No substantive information available due to confidential designation. Appears routine financial compliance with merger conditions established in approval process.

docket_entries.title: Public Comment - Individual Opposition (Outside Comment Period)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Individual public comment expressing generalized opposition to the transaction, characterizing it as 'selling out Minnesota.' Filed outside the formal comment period with no specific arguments, conditions, or relief requested. Contains no substantive analysis of transaction impacts.
key_arguments: Expresses general opposition characterizing transaction as harmful to Minnesota interests
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Procedurally deficient opposition filed outside comment period. Characterizes transaction as 'selling out Minnesota' with no specific arguments, conditions, or analysis of transaction impacts. Pure conclusory statement without evidentiary support.

docket_entries.title: Retail Customer Opposition - Private Equity Concerns
docket_entries.entry_summary: Individual customer opposes transaction on ideological grounds, expressing distrust of private industry ownership of utilities. Argues private industry 'will always find a way to take advantage of consumers, especially Black Rock' and that the sale will result in 'Minnesotans being screwed over.' No specific conditions offered that would make the transaction acceptable - opposition is categorical based on principle that utilities should not be privately owned. Filed outside the designated comment period.
key_arguments: Private industry ownership of utilities will inevitably harm Minnesota consumers regardless of conditions imposed
Private equity (specifically referencing Black Rock) cannot be trusted to operate utilities in the public interest
The transaction will result in environmental damage to Minnesota ('our beautiful state being scarred and abused')
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Introduces ideological distrust framework: 'private industry will always find a way to take advantage of consumers, especially Black Rock' and predicts 'Minnesotans being screwed over.' Establishes pattern of generalized private equity skepticism without specific evidence.
key_excerpts: No matter how air tight this damn sale is, private industry will always find a way to take advantage of consumers, especially Black Rock

docket_entries.title: Retail Customer Post-Approval Concern Letter
docket_entries.entry_summary: Minnesota Power customer Alyssia Kangas submits post-approval comment expressing concerns about BlackRock's acquisition despite Commission-imposed protections. She worries that 'profit motives could override the long-term interests of Minnesota Power customers and employees, despite the Commission's conditions.' Her specific concerns include: rate increases from acquisition or financial engineering, local job protection, maintaining clean energy investments, transparent compliance oversight, BlackRock's 10% Meta ownership creating campus influence, and impacts on electrical and nuclear grids. She requests the CAO's office conduct a legal review of the PUC decision and consider 'whether further oversight, intervention, or appeal may be needed to protect the public interest.' This appears to be a concerned citizen seeking recourse after approval rather than formal intervention.
key_arguments: Wall Street investors like BlackRock lack regional roots and profit motives could override customer and employee interests despite Commission conditions
Concern that rates could increase due to acquisition or related financial engineering
BlackRock's 10% ownership stake in Meta creates potential conflicts given Meta's data center presence in Minnesota Power service territory
Need for ongoing oversight to ensure Commission conditions remain transparent and enforceable
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Post-decision perspective acknowledging Commission protections exist but doubting effectiveness: 'profit motives could override the long-term interests of Minnesota Power customers and employees, despite the Commission's [protections].' First entry expressing concern despite regulatory safeguards.
key_excerpts: While I appreciate the Commission's efforts to include strong ratepayer protections, I am deeply worried about the potential impacts of this acquisition on our local communities, electric rates, jobs, and reliable utility service.

Major Wall Street investors like BlackRock do not have roots in our region. I fear that profit motives could override the long-term interests of Minnesota Power customers and employees, despite the Commission's conditions.

docket_entries.title: Batch of 5 Public Comments Opposing BlackRock Acquisition (Post-Decision)
docket_entries.entry_summary: Five Minnesota residents submitted comments opposing the completed BlackRock acquisition, all received after the October 3, 2025 approval decision and outside the formal comment period. The commenters express anger at the PUC's decision and raise common concerns: (1) Private equity ownership prioritizes investor returns over ratepayers ('primary responsibility is to deliver returns to investors — not to protect local communities'), (2) Loss of local control to 'distant boardrooms', (3) Expected future rate increases to recover acquisition costs, (4) Distrust of temporary promises that 'are not enforceable in the long run', (5) Concerns about selling public utilities to 'the highest bidder' and foreign entities (CPP). One commenter (Cocchiarella) directly questions how the PUC moved from skepticism to unanimous approval 'in just one week' and demands accountability mechanisms. Another (An) accuses Commissioner Sieben of personal financial gain ('How much payout are you getting'). The comments represent post-decision citizen dissatisfaction but have no procedural effect.
key_arguments: Private equity ownership puts profit over people: 'BlackRock and its affiliates are global investment firms whose primary responsibility is to deliver returns to investors — not to protect local communities or keep electricity affordable'

Rate increase concerns: 'Given the premium BlackRock and CPP are paying, it's reasonable to expect they'll seek to recover costs through higher rates. Minnesotans should not have to foot the bill for a Wall Street acquisition'

Loss of local control: 'decisions about our energy future may be made in distant boardrooms, not in the communities that rely on the service...We need a utility partner rooted in Minnesota, not one beholden to private investors scattered around the globe'

Promises are unenforceable: 'temporary promises around rate freezes and investment commitments...are not enforceable in the long run — and there's no guarantee they won't be reversed or weakened later'

Dangerous precedent for utility privatization: 'Minnesota's public utilities are up for sale to the highest bidder. We should be working to strengthen public oversight of essential services, not selling them off to massive investment firms'
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: Reiterates private equity profit prioritization concerns already raised in Entries #1, #2, #5. Expresses anger at completed decision but presents no new substantive arguments beyond general opposition to BlackRock ownership.
key_excerpts: BlackRock is the world's largest asset manager...and doesn't have any skin in the game when it comes to the needs of those in northern Minnesota.

We need clarity and transparency from the MNPUC as to why they believe that allowing BlackRock to acquire MN Power is good for ratepayers...We have sat here and listened to each commission member explain how in just one week, all their skepticism and fears had been erased.
An administrative law judge reviewing the deal found it posed a net harm to the public interest, and that should carry serious weight.

docket_entries.title: Resident Kristy M. Opposes BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: Individual ratepayer opposes the acquisition based on general concerns about private equity ownership, arguing that 'Private equity firms like BlackRock are notorious for putting profits for executives and shareholders ahead of the needs of communities.' Cites Sierra Club representative Jenna Yeakle's statement that the deal 'will force ratepayers to be beholden to the private equity agenda.' Opposition is based on principle rather than specific transaction details - concerned about reduced transparency, increased rates, and accountability to investors rather than Minnesota residents. No specific conditions would satisfy this opposition; wants outright rejection to ensure 'Minnesota's energy future is controlled by those who have the public interest...at heart—not by distant private equity firms.'
key_arguments: Private equity firms prioritize profits for executives and shareholders over community needs
Transaction will reduce utility accountability to Minnesota residents and increase accountability to corporate investors
Deal risks increased rates, reduced transparency, and service decisions driven by investor returns rather than reliable and affordable power
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Establishes boilerplate opposition language: 'Private equity firms like BlackRock are notorious for putting profits for executives and shareholders ahead of the needs of communities.' First to cite external advocacy source (Sierra Club) to support claims.
key_excerpts: Private equity firms like BlackRock are notorious for putting profits for executives and shareholders ahead of the needs of communities. Handing control of a critical energy provider to a firm with no direct accountability to Minnesota residents risks increased rates, reduced transparency, and service decisions that prioritize investor returns over reliable and affordable access to power.
If the deal goes through, it will force ratepayers to be beholden to the private equity agenda.

docket_entries.title: Public Comment Opposing BlackRock Acquisition
docket_entries.entry_summary: Individual citizen Bonnie Johnson opposes the acquisition based on concerns about BlackRock's reputation and ownership structure. She characterizes BlackRock as 'not a reputable company for the people' and 'completely owned by the oligarchy,' urging the Commission to reject the transaction if concerned about Minnesota residents. The comment was received outside the official comment period and provides no specific allegations regarding utility service, rates, reliability, or other traditional regulatory concerns.
key_arguments: BlackRock is not a reputable company and is 'completely owned by the oligarchy'
Allowing BlackRock into Minnesota is not in the interest of the people of Minnesota
docket_entries.cumulative_impact: NEW: Introduces reputation-based opposition framing BlackRock as 'not a reputable company for the people' and 'completely owned by the oligarchy.' Establishes narrative foundation focused on corporate ownership structure rather than specific utility impacts.
key_excerpts: Black rock is not a reputable company for the people. If you are concerned about the people of Minnesota, you would stay away from black rock, which is completely owned by the oligarchy.
